title: 🎙️ Science, Wonder & the Natural World
description: Today we look at how Cariboo and rural communities are building lasting resilience — adapting to economic transitions, climate pressures, and the challenges of isolation. From off-grid energy and community broadband to post-resource-economy diversification and cooperative models, these are the stories about how rural BC is not just surviving change but shaping its own future. - Daily themed article selection for podcast generation

_podcast.theme_description: Today we look at how Cariboo and rural communities are building lasting resilience — adapting to economic transitions, climate pressures, and the challenges of isolation. From off-grid energy and community broadband to post-resource-economy diversification and cooperative models, these are the stories about how rural BC is not just surviving change but shaping its own future.
_podcast.theme_scoring_prompt: Rate how well this article fits the theme 'Resilient Rural Futures' — stories about how Cariboo and rural communities are adapting, diversifying, and building lasting strength. Strong fits include: post-resource-economy transition and community economic planning (post-mill, post-mine, post-forestry towns finding new paths), local economic diversification initiatives and community futures discussions, rural workforce transition and skills development, co-operative and community-ownership economic models, off-grid power systems and solar for Cariboo homes, mesh networks and community-owned connectivity, self-hosted systems and data sovereignty, telehealth platforms and remote healthcare tech, climate adaptation technology for rural areas, emergency communication systems and disaster tech, renewable energy innovation, food security tech (greenhouses, vertical farms, local food tracking), water monitoring and conservation systems, affordable housing innovation, resilient infrastructure design, rural remote work tools and digital nomad infrastructure, and future-focused solutions that help Cariboo communities thrive despite isolation or climate challenges. Score equally highly for community economic resilience stories and technology resilience stories — both are core to rural futures. Score 0-100 where 100 is a perfect thematic match and 0 is completely unrelated.

items.title: [New Atlas] Nanogenerator harvests electricity from evaporating seawater
items.content_html:
Imagine a device using an energy source the size of planet Earth, and offering nearly limitless electricity with no ecologically devastating mining required to get it. Science fiction? A lie from Big Battery? A fentanyl fantasy from the Energizer Bunny? No – it’s real, and it’s the ocean.

items.title: [Kottke.org] The Leaf Sheep Slug: The Animal That Eats Sunshine
items.content_html:
This is an animal called the leaf sheep:

It’s a species of slug that is partially solar-powered, like a plant. Leaf sheep are kleptoplastic organisms that steal chloroplasts from algae, store them in their bodies, and then can rely on photosynthesis for their energy needs:

The Costasiella sea slug not only looks like a succulent—it acts like one, too. One of the few animals able to photosynthesize, this tiny invertebrate (also known as the leaf slug or leaf sheep) acquires chloroplasts by munching on Avrainvillea, a paddle-shaped seaweed with a velvety texture. It then stores those chloroplasts in its own body, which enables the slug to soak up sunlight and transform it into energy—a process that also gives the mollusk its green color.

The chloroplasts are stored in the horn-shaped structures called cerata located on the slugs’ backs. Cerata evolved to increase the surface area of these animals for use in respiration and surface area is very helpful if you run on solar panels.

And they’re also cute as a button! I mean, look at these things:
